---
title: "Differential Gene Expression Analysis to Pathway Enrichment:Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome data set"
author: "hec and nikki"
format: html
editor: visual
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE)
library(collaborativeproject)
```

**Overview**

This vignette walks through a complete bioinformatics workflow applied to a publicly available differential gene expression (DEG) dataset from **vascular Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome (vEDS)** skin fibroblasts. The workflow has three main steps:

1.  **Load** the DEG table from a public repository
2.  **Visualize** differential expression using a volcano plot
3.  **Identify biological pathways** overrepresented among the DEGs using KEGG enrichment analysis

Each section explains what the code is doing and why, so you can follow along even if this is your first time working with transcriptomic data.

**Background: What is vEDS?**

Vascular Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome (vEDS) is a rare and severe connective tissue disorder caused by mutations in the **COL3A1** gene, which encodes **type III collagen** — a structural protein critical for the integrity of blood vessels, skin, and internal organs. The disease is associated with a high risk of spontaneous arterial rupture, making it one of the most life-threatening subtypes of EDS.

Understanding which genes are dysregulated in vEDS fibroblasts can reveal the molecular mechanisms underlying the disease and point toward potential therapeutic targets.

```{r collagen_graphic, echo=FALSE, fig.cap="Left = normal colllagen right = vEDS collagen", out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/hjleon19/collaborative-project/master/Collagen.png")
```

**Dataset Information**

The data used in this analysis comes from a published microarray study comparing skin fibroblasts from vEDS patients with those from healthy controls. Raw and processed data are publicly available in the GEO database under accession [**GSE102042**](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/geo/query/acc.cgi?acc=GSE102042).

**Participants**

The study included **12 individuals: 3 vEDS patients and 9 age-matched healthy controls**. Each vEDS patient carried a distinct dominant-negative structural mutation in COL3A1:

| Patient | Mutation | Type |
|----|----|----|
| P1 | c.709G\>A (p.Gly237Arg) | Missense |
| P2 | c.951+6T\>C | Splice site (in-frame skipping of exon 14) |
| P3 | c.1835G\>A (p.Gly612Asp) | Missense |

All mutations affect glycine residues within the collagen triple-helix domain or alter splicing, disrupting the structural integrity of the collagen triple helix.

**Cell Type and Culture**

-   **Cell type:** Skin fibroblasts derived from skin punch biopsies
-   **Culture medium:** Earle's Modified Eagle Medium (MEM) with 2 mM L-glutamine, 10% FBS, and 100 μg/ml penicillin/streptomycin
-   **Conditions:** 37°C, 5% CO₂
-   **Passage:** Cells were harvested between the **3rd and 6th passage** after expansion to full confluency using 0.25% trypsin / 0.02% EDTA

**Transcriptome Profiling**

Gene expression was measured using the **Affymetrix Gene 1.0 ST** microarray platform. RNA was extracted using the **Qiagen RNeasy kit** and quality was assessed on an **Agilent 2100 BioAnalyzer**. From 250 ng of total RNA per sample, labeled targets were prepared and hybridized to the arrays for 16 hours at 45°C.

Raw array data were processed using **Partek Genomics Suite** with the following pipeline:

1.  **Background correction and normalization:** Robust Multiarray Average (RMA)
2.  **Transformation:** Data converted to log₂ scale
3.  **Quality control:** Principal Component Analysis (PCA) used to identify outliers and batch effects

**DEG Selection Criteria**

To be included in the final DEG table, a gene had to meet **all three** of the following criteria:

-   **Statistical test:** One-way ANOVA with $p < 0.05$
-   **Fold-change threshold:** $\geq 1.5$-fold change (or $\leq -1.5$)
-   **Multiple testing correction:** Benjamini–Hochberg (BH) FDR $< 0.05$

This yielded **969 DEGs total: 281 up-regulated and 688 down-regulated** in vEDS patients relative to controls. Microarray results were validated via **qPCR** using the $2^{-(\Delta\Delta Ct)}$ method, normalized against four reference genes: HPRT, GAPDH, ATP5B, and CYC1.

**Step 1 — Load Data**

**What this function does**

`load_deg_table()` reads the DEG table from a local file or a URL. It handles both comma-separated (`.csv`) and tab-delimited (`.txt`) files and tries UTF-8 encoding first, falling back to latin1 for files with special characters.

After reading the file, it:

-   **Skips the title row** (row 1 in the file) and uses row 2 as the header
-   **Standardizes column names** to `Row`, `TranscriptID`, `gene_assignment`, `GeneSymbol`, `RefSeq`, `padj`, and `logFC` — regardless of what the original file uses
-   **Converts** `padj` and `logFC` to numeric and **removes any rows with missing values** in either column
-   **Prints a summary** so you can verify the data loaded correctly

> **Why skip row 1?** The S3 Table file has a human-readable title on the first line (e.g., *"S3 Table. Full list of DEGs..."*). `skip = 1` tells R to ignore that line before reading the column headers.

```{r load}
github_url <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/hjleon19/collaborative-project/refs/heads/master/S3%20Table.txt"
deg <- load_deg_table(github_url)
```

After running this chunk, `deg` is a data frame with **969 rows** (one per DEG) and 7 columns. The `logFC` column contains log₂ fold-change values (positive = higher expression in vEDS, negative = lower), and `padj` contains BH-corrected p-values.

**Step 2 — Volcano Plot**

**What is a volcano plot?**

A volcano plot is a standard visualization in differential expression analysis. Each point represents one gene:

-   **X-axis:** log₂ fold change — how much expression changed between vEDS and controls. Positive values = up-regulated in vEDS; negative = down-regulated.
-   **Y-axis:** $-\log_{10}(\text{FDR-adjusted p-value})$ — statistical significance. The more significant a gene, the **higher** it appears on the plot.

Genes that are both statistically significant **and** strongly up or down-regulated appear in the upper left and upper right "arms" of the volcano shape.

```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.cap="Example of how to read a volcano plot. Genes in the upper corners are the most biologically interesting — they show both a large change in expression and high statistical confidence.", out.width="60%"}
knitr::include_graphics("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/YOUR_USERNAME/YOUR_REPO/refs/heads/master/volcano_explainer.png")
```

**What this function does**

`volcano_plot()` takes the DEG data frame and plots only the genes that pass both a fold-change and a significance threshold. Key parameters:

| Parameter | Value used | Meaning |
|--------------------------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| `logFC` | `"logFC"` | Column name for fold-change values |
| `pval` | `"padj"` | Column name for p-values |
| `alpha` | `0.05` | Significance threshold (dashed horizontal line) |
| `lfc_thresh` | `1` | Minimum \|log₂FC\| to color a point (dashed vertical lines) |
| `already_adjusted` | `TRUE` | Skips BH correction — our p-values are already FDR-adjusted in the S3 table |

The function classifies each gene as **up-regulated** (red), **down-regulated** (blue), or **not significant** (NC, not plotted). The count of genes in each direction is printed to the console and annotated on the plot.

> **Note:** Setting `already_adjusted = TRUE` is important here. If you set it to `FALSE`, the function would apply BH correction a second time to values that are already corrected, making the thresholds overly conservative.

```{r volcano}
volcano_plot(
  data             = deg,
  logFC            = "logFC",   # column name for fold change
  pval             = "padj",    # column name for p-value
  alpha            = 0.05,      # significance threshold
  lfc_thresh       = 1,         # minimum fold change to show
  already_adjusted = TRUE,      # p-values are already BH-corrected in S3 table
  title            = "Volcano Plot: vEDS vs Controls"
)
```

The resulting plot shows a pronounced downward shift — the larger number of blue (down-regulated) genes is consistent with the 688 down vs. 281 up breakdown reported in the original paper.

**Step 3 — Pathway Analysis**

**Why pathway analysis?**

A list of 969 individual genes is hard to interpret biologically. **Pathway enrichment analysis** asks a higher-level question: are the genes in our list distributed randomly across known biological pathways, or do they cluster in specific processes more than we'd expect by chance?

We use **KEGG (Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes)**, a curated database of biological pathways, to test this statistically. This is done using the `enrichKEGG()` function from the `clusterProfiler` package, which performs a **Fisher's exact test** for each pathway and corrects for multiple testing using the BH method.

> **Important distinction:** The top 10 up/down-regulated genes printed below are sorted purely by fold-change magnitude for display purposes — they are **not** the input to the pathway enrichment. The enrichment runs on the **entire filtered DEG list** (all genes with `padj < 0.01` and `|logFC| > 0.2`). The pathways that emerge are determined entirely by which genes are in your data. Running this on a different dataset would produce different pathways.

**What this function does**

`pathway_analysis()` carries out four steps:

1.  **Filters** the DEG table to genes meeting a stricter FDR threshold (`padj < 0.01`) and a minimum fold-change (`|logFC| > 0.2`). These thresholds focus enrichment on the most confident results.
2.  **Prints** the top `n` up and down-regulated genes sorted by fold-change magnitude.
3.  **Maps** gene symbols (e.g., `COL3A1`) to numeric **Entrez IDs**, which KEGG requires. This step uses the `org.Hs.eg.db` human genome annotation database. Non-coding RNAs (e.g., SNORD, RNU genes) often fail to map — this is expected and normal.
4.  **Runs KEGG enrichment** and returns the top `n_pathways` results ranked by adjusted p-value, along with a named vector of logFC values that `pathview` will use to color pathway diagrams.

```{r pathway_run}
library(pathview)
library(org.Hs.eg.db)
library(AnnotationDbi)
library(clusterProfiler)

pathway_results <- pathway_analysis(
  deg         = deg,
  top_n       = 10,    # number of top up/down genes to print
  n_pathways  = 3,     # number of top KEGG pathways to return
  padj_cutoff = 0.01,  # stricter FDR threshold for enrichment input
  lfc_cutoff  = 0.2    # minimum |logFC| for enrichment input
)
```

**How pathways are ranked**

Pathways are ranked by their **BH-adjusted p-value** from the Fisher's exact test. A pathway ranks highly when a disproportionately large fraction of your filtered DEGs belong to it relative to the total size of that pathway in the KEGG database. The `n_pathways = 3` argument simply slices the top 3 from this ranked list — it does not influence the statistics themselves.

**Step 4 — Pathway Maps**

`pathview` downloads the KEGG pathway diagrams and overlays your logFC values directly onto them. Genes are colored on the diagram:

-   **Red** = up-regulated in vEDS
-   **Green/Blue** = down-regulated in vEDS
-   **White/grey** = gene is in the pathway but not in your DEG list

The diagrams are saved as PNG files in your working directory with the suffix `vEDS_1_`, `vEDS_2_`, and `vEDS_3_` followed by the pathway name.

> **Why is `pathview` called here and not inside `pathway_analysis()`?** `pathview` requires its internal data objects to be available via `library(pathview)` at the top level of the session. Calling it from inside a package function can cause it to fail silently, so we call it directly in the vignette after loading the library.

```{r pathview_run, results='hide', message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
for (i in seq_len(nrow(pathway_results$top_pathways))) {
  pathview(
    gene.data  = pathway_results$gene_data,
    pathway.id = pathway_results$top_pathways$ID[i],
    species    = "hsa",
    out.suffix = paste0("vEDS_", i, "_",
                        gsub(" ", "_", pathway_results$top_pathways$Description[i])),
    kegg.native = TRUE
  )
}
```

The three pathway maps are saved to your working directory. To display them inline here, they have been uploaded to the repository!

```{r pathway-map-1, echo=FALSE, fig.cap="Top enriched KEGG pathway — logFC values overlaid on the KEGG diagram. Red = up-regulated, blue/green = down-regulated in vEDS.", out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/hjleon19/collaborative-project/master/hsa04110.vEDS_1_Cell_cycle.png")
```

```{r pathway-map-2, echo=FALSE, fig.cap="Second enriched KEGG pathway.", out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/hjleon19/collaborative-project/master/hsa03030.vEDS_2_DNA_replication.png")
```

```{r pathway-map-3, echo=FALSE, fig.cap="Third enriched KEGG pathway.", out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/hjleon19/collaborative-project/master/hsa03430.vEDS_3_Mismatch_repair.png")
```

**Summary**

This workflow demonstrates how to move from a raw list of DEGs to interpretable biological conclusions:

| Step | Function | Input | Output |
|------------------|-------------------|------------------|------------------|
| Load data | `load_deg_table()` | GitHub URL | 969-row DEG data frame |
| Visualize | `volcano_plot()` | DEG data frame | Colored volcano plot |
| Enrichment | `pathway_analysis()` | DEG data frame | Top 3 KEGG pathways + logFC vector |
| Pathway maps | `pathview()` | logFC vector + pathway IDs | PNG diagrams |

The pathways identified here are driven entirely by the DEGs in the vEDS dataset — running the same code on a different disease dataset would produce different enriched pathways, reflecting the biology of that condition.
